Jeff: So, this is Nicholas Wilton. We're going to break down his launch of his program
he calls Art2Life. Art2Life is a program where he teaches how to make art.
Nicholas is a painter, an accomplished painter. He's done some in-person
workshops, but he had no online experience, no online marketing experience, had
a very small list. He just joined PLF and got right down to it and had an amazing
launch. An amazing launch, considering it was starting from scratch, no
experience, teaching a ... How to make art isn't like one of those ... What you
think of as one of the great big mine niches, and yet his launch did over
$400,000. It's pretty amazing.
So, we're going to break down PLC1 in this video. I'm just going to show you a
little clip of Nicholas so you can get a feel for him, and I will warn you, the audio
of this clip's not going to be great, just by the virtue that we're capturing it, but
we're also giving you his entire video, so you can actually watch his entire video.
Here, I'm going to break down ... Mostly I'm going to be going through the
transcript of his video, but I just wanted to show you a little bit of the video just to
get you a feel for Nick.
Nicholas: Hi. Today I want to talk about the creative process and how you can get your
work to be made more easily, be more powerful, and be more like you. My name's
Nicholas Wilton. I'm a fine artist. We're standing in my studio in Sausalito. I've
been painting my entire life and making art my whole life. I've worked with
thousands of people, helping them bring their work out to make their work more
potent, more powerful.
Jeff: So, as you can see, first of all, I think Nick's got great presence on camera, but
he's not super polished. He's got lots of ums in here, he's got lots of ahs in here.
Not super, super polished, and frankly, not unlike me in the way he presents. I
have plenty of ums and ahs as well. Less so when I'm scripted, but I absolutely
do. So, you don't have to be uber professional when you're presenting. I mean, if
you look at his apron, it's got a twist in it around his neck. It would look better if
it was off his collar. It doesn't matter. What matters is you bringing the authentic
you, and he is ... I think he's got great presence and he knows what he's talking
about. I like the set. It's lit properly. I like the way he came on camera. If we go
back to that.
Nicholas: Hi. Today I want to talk about the creative process.

Jeff: Yeah, there's nothing that screams slick professional salesman here, and he's not.
But I really, really like his presence. So, now, let's head on over and take a look at
his script. I said script, but this is a transcript. I'm not sure if Nicholas was
working from a script or not. This is the transcript, and you can see as we go
through there, there'll be time stamps, like right here [shown in video], that's the
30-second mark. So, when he starts off, he does a great job of introducing
himself. Just comes out and says hi, introduces himself standing in his studio.
“I've been painting my entire life, making art my whole life, worked with
thousands of people, helping them bringing their work out to make their work
more potent, more powerful, and more like themselves.”
That, right over here, you can see my note [shown in video]. It's just an
introduction, and it's positioning for himself, and it's building authority to say
he's worked with all these people. Doesn't say he's worked with them online
because he hasn't. He's worked with them offline. He's helped people offline, but
he's never taught online. Then right in here he starts to position the pain,
the problem of the viewer that they have as an artist, and he starts to
foreshadow the transformation. “This happened quite by accident. It
actually happened through my own struggle early on. I was always trying to make
work ....” This is a transcript, so transcripts aren't always perfect English, and if
you go back when he delivers this line, he pulls it off just fine, but here in the
transcript it looks awkward.
But “I was always trying to make it work and I struggled. I struggled a lot to try to
make my work powerful and actually just make work that I liked.” So, he's
starting to talk about that pain. Then here, he brings out that pain quite a bit
more. He defines it better in this part, and this is ... There's an old
copywriting term called dimensionalizing, and this is ... It's sort of
where you give it dimension and you give it shape and you fill it out.
One day you make something that's really great, and sometimes it can get done so
easily, then you work two more days, then you make a few more things and those
are terrible, then you feel like giving up, then you get frustrated, then you make
something else and it looks great. Then you do something you hate.
So, go back and watch how he delivers this, and I just ... His delivery of this was
great, and it was super authentic and super real, and you could tell that he was
speaking it from experience. Then down here he talks about the discovery of
the solution. “I love the creative process, but it really didn't get going, it didn't
happen for me until I started to pay attention to the process of my work and what
was actually happening.”

Then down here “I started to take notes when things were working and when
things weren't,” and he took notes “over decades”, over “20 years of writing it
down.” So, this is building the value of what he's about to deliver. How did this
happen? How could I be so good one day, and bad the next day? Again, he's tying
it back to the problem, and that's when he came up with the system, and that's
when he came up with the Art2Life principles.
Now, he continues down here. This is where he starts to talk about the
solution. Here he talks about the discovery of the solution, and now he gets a
little more into the nitty gritty of what the solution is. He's not selling what it is,
he's just talking about it and he's setting up the teaching of the solution. So, it's
the key pieces. Not like he's in sales mode here, he's just giving the reason why
he's about to teach this stuff.
It says, "I've been teaching this for 20 years now, and it's evolved through a lot of
trial and error." Of course there's trial and error. There's a lot of pain
there. It took him a lot of pain to get to the solution, which, of course,
makes it more intriguing and more valuable. “There's some complexity here, but
if you can get these principles, you can transform your work.” “When I first
started teaching this, I could take someone that was vaguely, minorly interested
in art, had no experience,” and they could actually start creating powerful stuff.
Again, you should definitely go check out the video and how he talks about it. It's
really strong. “I'm not interested in teaching a set of techniques or teaching a way
of working or working with certain materials,” but he also talks about these
“principles, these ideas that are bigger than the materials.”
So, that's all the setup for the solution, and he keeps on going on here and talking
a little bit more, setting up the solution. Then down in here he starts to set up the
actual teaching in a stronger way. He talks about exactly what he's going to be
teaching, and down in here is where he digs into the teaching. And he really goes
deep. So, this is about ... Somewhere in here, you could say this is the start of the
teaching at 4 minutes and 30 seconds, or you could say it starts here at 5 minutes.
Somewhere in this 4 and a half to 5 and a half minute mark, he starts teaching,
and then he just goes on.
He teaches, and he teaches deeply. If you watch the videos, he's actually
demonstrating it; he's actually painting and demonstrating. Still teaching down
here at the 12-minute mark, and he's still teaching down here. It's been about 12
minutes of teaching, and it's good, strong teaching. He knows his teaching, he has
been teaching this stuff offline, and he knows what can get results.

Down here he starts to transition into the conclusion. Okay, so he's been
teaching, teaching, teaching. We're up to about 12 to ... I don't know, 12 to 13,
maybe 14 minutes of teaching. Now, here's the transition.
“Okay, so when I learned about this ... When I learned this about design, it was
really helpful for me. So, if this sounds new to you, if you haven't heard of this or
it's coming to you in a new way, it's really great because this is an opportunity to
take it. Take this information and it's really powerful.” Now, in this whole video,
the one thing I don't love is I don't love his overall conclusion. He hits the pieces
he needs to hit, but it feels a little abrupt to me to be going 18 minutes and then
just to wrap this up in a minute or so, minute, minute and a half. But this is
where he starts to transition into the conclusion.
Here's where he foreshadows the next video. “In the upcoming video, we're
really going to be diving into value. Design and value work together, and those
are really, really powerful. They're by far the most powerful tools that you can use
to change your work. It's amazing.” Okay, that's good foreshadowing, I liked it.
Then this is the CTA, the call to action, is what CTA means, the call to
action. For him, it's a call for comments, which is great. “Please ask questions, I'm
super into answering them below, and feedback. If you ask a question and you're
not sure, because I've gone over something too quickly or it's a little murky. It's
really helpful for everyone.”
So, this just feels a little jumbly here, and it feels like he just piled it all in. It's
like, here's a transition, here's a foreshadow, here's a call to action. It just feels a
little bit all piled in here, and then here he goes back to foreshadowing. I liked
this last piece, but in here, I just think probably he took a few more seconds in
here and tried to be a little bit more articulate here, then I think it would be
stronger. First of all, the proof's in the pudding. This launch did $400,000. This
video got lots and lots of comments. People loved it. So, it was a great video. It
was a great video.
The ‘do differently’ is I probably would spend a little more time here and put a
little more time. Frankly, ending videos is the thing that people struggle with the
most. It's the hardest thing, and trust me, I struggle with it just like everyone else
does. So, it is good to give some forethought into how you are going to end your
videos. But at the very end of it, the very, very end, is a nice call to action. A nice
call to action. I'm sorry, a nice foreshadowing of the next video.
